control of one’s image, name and reputation, and over the public disclosure of

information about oneself, they are speaking to these selfsame continental sensibilities.

To be sure, they are talking about “privacy” in a way that many Americans also talk

about it. The idea that “privacy” is really about the control of one’s public image has

long appealed to the most philosophically sophisticated American commentators, from
58
     Whitman Harsh Justice.

20
 Alan Westin, 59 to Charles Fried, 60 to Jeffrey Rosen, 61 to Thomas Nagel. 62 Moreover, it is

an idea that can already be found in the most famous of American articles, Warren and

Brandeis’ 1890 “The Right to Privacy.”63 All of these American writers have viewed the

danger in the violation of our “privacy” as the danger that we will lose the capacity to

control what Goffman famously called our “presentation of self,” our image before the

eyes of others in society. All of them have thought of our right to privacy, perhaps a

shade paradoxically, as our right to a public image of our own making, as the right of

control of our public face. Indeed, it is precisely for that reason that they have insisted on

the connection between privacy and personhood.

So the prevailing continental conception is also the one that most thoughtful

Americans commentators on privacy and “personhood” have found the wisest and most

sophisticated. But if this conception has triumphed in continental law, it is not because

European lawyers possess any unique measure of wisdom or sophistication. Law is not

about the realization in this world of wisdom or sophistication as such. Law is about

what works, what seems appealing and appropriate in a given society, and the conception

of privacy as control of one’s “image” has succeeded because it fits into continental

social traditions, and into a quotidian continental culture of respect. Continental privacy

is “continental” in much the way that continental hate speech law is “continental,” in

much the way that continental prison law is “continental”—or for that matter, in much

the way that continental etiquette is “continental.”

   Westin, Privacy and Freedom (1967).
60
   Fried, Privacy, 77 Yale L.J. 475 (1968).
61

62

Freedom and Respect for Persons, in Nomos XIII: Privacy (J. Ronald Pennock & J.W. Chapman,eds.,
1971).
63
   4 Harvard L. Rev. 193 (1890).

21
         Indeed, etiquette makes, as so often, a striking example. It is not an accident that

both etiquette and privacy law show the same anxious preoccupation with “public

image.” Thus it is common for continental etiquette guides to open with a section called

“how we present ourselves before the world”64 ; or “the politesse of appearances”65 ; or

more broadly a section on how to maintain the correct external look and manners. 66

Rules about how to dress and how to wear makeup are part of continental etiquette just as
